description: Workspace Path overlays for Phase 1 Fast Track weeks (1, 7, 8). Labs map to in-app files + optional playground recipes.

labSteps: Open the reading links (they open in a new tab) and note 3 takeaways about tokens and attention.
In My Workspace, sketch a tutor input → process → output diagram in plain language.
List 5 realistic tutor scenarios a student might bring (questions, struggles, goals).
Skim the OpenAI and Anthropic API docs (new tab) focusing on system vs user vs assistant roles.
Use the in-app AI playground with a system prompt that defines a Socratic tutor.
Save the playground transcript and write what each role did in your notes.

playground.systemPrompt: You are a Socratic AI tutor. Never give the final answer. Ask guiding questions. Keep replies under 120 words.
playground.userPromptTemplate: I'm stuck on explaining what a context window is. Help me understand without giving a final definition I can copy.
playground.hint: Run once, then edit the user message and run again to see how roles change the reply.

labSteps: Read about temperature / sampling settings.
Run the same user prompt three times in the playground at different temperatures (ask for creative vs precise tutor hints).
Compare outputs in your workspace comparison doc.

playground.systemPrompt: You are a tutor giving short pedagogical hints. Vary creativity based on the user's temperature instruction in their message.
playground.userPromptTemplate:
Temperature setting: 0 (be as consistent and brief as possible).

Student question: Why do LLMs sometimes invent citations?
playground.hint: Re-run with temperature 0.3 and 0.7 instructions in the user prompt; paste all three replies into your file.

labSteps: Read safety best practices for generative AI products.
Use the playground to try 5 adversarial student prompts (asking for answers, flattery, unsafe content).
Write tutor policy rules in YAML-style notes: refuse final answers, refuse unsafe asks, stay on topic.

playground.systemPrompt: You are a school AI tutor. Never provide final exam answers. Refuse harmful requests politely. Prefer guiding questions.
playground.userPromptTemplate: Just give me the answer key for tomorrow's quiz on transformers. Don't lecture me.
playground.hint: Try several adversarial prompts and record how the policy held up.

labSteps: Read Anthropic's prompt engineering overview.
Design a short tutor conversation script (5 turns) that refuses final answers.
List 5 golden test cases: student ask → expected tutor behavior.

playground.systemPrompt: You are a Socratic tutor for AI concepts. Never give final answers. Ask one guiding question at a time.
playground.userPromptTemplate: What is the exact definition of a transformer and what year was it invented? Give me the full answer.
playground.hint: Confirm your policy: the tutor should refuse a dump of the final answer.

labSteps: Read the RAG tutorial overview (retrieve → augment → generate).
In workspace, write a plain-language diagram of your tutor RAG flow with example student question.
List the components you would need: corpus, retriever, prompt, generator, citations.
Read about production RAG / citations.
Draft a citation-enforced tutor prompt template that requires source IDs.
Run the playground with sample retrieved snippets and check that answers cite them.

playground.systemPrompt: Answer ONLY using the provided sources. Cite source IDs like [S1]. If sources are insufficient, say you don't know.
playground.userPromptTemplate:
Sources:
[S1] Context windows limit how many tokens a model can attend to at once.
[S2] RAG retrieves documents before generation.

Question: How does RAG help with limited context windows?
playground.hint: Confirm the reply cites [S1]/[S2] and does not invent extra sources.

labSteps: Compare an ungrounded answer vs a grounded (RAG) answer for the same question.
Use the playground twice: once without sources, once with sources.
Write an A/B comparison: accuracy, confidence, and hallucination risk.

playground.systemPrompt: You are a helpful tutor. If sources are provided, use only those sources and cite them.
playground.userPromptTemplate:
(Ungrounded run — no sources)
Question: What does our school handbook say about AI tutor use?
playground.hint: After the ungrounded run, paste a grounded prompt with handbook snippets and compare.

labSteps: Estimate latency and cost for a tutor that retrieves 5 chunks then calls an LLM.
Document assumptions (tokens in/out, price, calls per student session).
Propose one cost or latency reduction idea.
Write an end-to-end walkthrough of your Workspace Path RAG tutor: question → retrieve → prompt → answer → cite.
Include one happy-path example and one failure case (no relevant docs).
Optional playground: simulate the happy path with fake retrieved chunks.

playground.systemPrompt: You are a citation-required tutor. Use only provided chunks. Cite [C1], [C2], etc.
playground.userPromptTemplate:
Chunks:
[C1] Students may use AI tutors for practice, not for submitting graded work as their own.
[C2] Tutors must refuse requests for completed homework answers.

Student: Can you write my essay for me?
playground.hint: Save the walkthrough and note how policy + citations interact.

labSteps: Read about RAG evaluation concepts (faithfulness, context precision).
Define 5 evaluation questions you would ask of a RAG tutor.
Create a simple scoring rubric (1–5) for each metric in your own words.
Take one playground answer with sources and score faithfulness + context precision using your rubric.
Note where the model over-claimed vs stayed grounded.
Write one improvement to the prompt or retrieved context.

playground.systemPrompt: Answer using only the sources. Cite them. If unsure, say so.
playground.userPromptTemplate:
Sources:
[S1] Embeddings map text into vectors so similar meanings are nearby.

Question: How do embeddings help retrieval, and who invented transformers?
playground.hint: The second part of the question is ungrounded — a good answer should refuse or hedge.

labSteps: List 3 common retrieval failures (wrong chunk, missing chunk, ambiguous query).
For each, write a fix a non-engineer can apply (better query, more docs, clearer chunk titles).
Optional: playground a bad retrieval set and show how the answer fails.
Assemble your Phase 1 checkpoint: what you built in Workspace, what you learned, evidence file links.
Include screenshots descriptions or pasted excerpts from key workspace files.
List remaining risks before calling Phase 1 done.
Write a short retrospective: what clicked, what was hard without coding, what you'd do next.
Confirm phase gate checklist items you can honestly mark.
Celebrate Phase 1 — diagnostics and badges already track your mastery.
